title: Common Neonatal Orthopedic Conditions
authors: Katherine M. Schroeder, Maryse L. Bouchard & Klane K. White

blocks.text:
ทำไม neonatologist ต้องเข้าใจ Common Neonatal Orthopedic Conditions?
Orthopedic conditions ใน newborn ไม่คุกคามชีวิตโดยตรง แต่ถ้าพลาด → functional impairment ตลอดชีวิต. สิ่งที่ neonatologist ต้องทำทุกราย: (1) Ortolani + Barlow test ทุก newborn exam — DDH 11.5:1000, frank dislocation 1–2:1000; (2) ห้าม double-diapering — ไม่ใช่ treatment DDH; ใช้ Pavlik harness; (3) CMT พบ 0.3–2%: ตรวจ SCM ทุกราย — stretching เริ่มเร็วได้ผลดีมาก; (4) Clubfoot → Ponseti method เริ่มภายในสัปดาห์แรก; (5) Brachial plexus palsy: biceps recovery ที่ 3 เดือน = prognostic landmark; (6) Neonatal septic arthritis: CRP มี NPV 95%, blood culture positive เพียง 50% — อย่ารอ culture กลับก่อน drain.
เป้าหมายการเรียนรู้วันนี้
ทำ Ortolani และ Barlow test ได้ถูกต้อง แยก clunk จาก click; รู้ความหมายของ Galeazzi sign
รู้ DDH screening guidelines (AAOS 2014/2015): universal vs selective ultrasound, Pavlik harness success rates
แยก foot deformities ได้: calcaneovalgus vs CVT vs metatarsus adductus vs clubfoot — ทั้ง features และ treatment
รู้ Ponseti method สำหรับ clubfoot: จำนวน cast, tenotomy, bracing timeline
วินิจฉัย CMT: pseudotumor vs muscular vs postural torticollis; แยก Klippel-Feil, Sandifer syndrome
รู้ physiologic genu varum timeline (Salenius & Vankka 1975) และเมื่อไหร่ต้องกังวล
แยก tibial bowing: posteromedial (NF1 ≠) vs anterolateral (NF1 40–80%) vs anteromedial (fibular hemimelia)
รู้ congenital vertebral malformations: failure of formation vs segmentation, association screening (renal 37%, cardiac 15%)
รู้ obstetric birth fractures: clavicle 2–3:1000, treatment; OI Sillence type II vs III; brachial plexus palsy — Erb vs Klumpke vs global
วินิจฉัยและรักษา neonatal osteomyelitis/septic arthritis: CRP NPV 95%, organisms, drainage indications
1. Developmental Dysplasia of the Hip (DDH)
1.1 ภาพรวมและ Epidemiology
DDH = spectrum: acetabular dysplasia → unstable but located hip → frank dislocation (complete loss of femoral head–acetabulum contact)
Incidence: 11.5 per 1000 infants; frank dislocation 1–2 per 1000
Left hip 60%, right hip 20%, bilateral 20%
Risk factors: breech positioning, family history, limited hip abduction, talipes, female sex, swaddling, large birth size, first-born
Natural history: may resolve spontaneously หรือ progress → subluxation → precocious arthritis ใน young adults; late diagnosis = devastating sequelae
Hips can be divided: syndromic (myelodysplasia, arthrogryposis, Larsen syndrome — wk 12–18 gestation) vs typical (otherwise healthy, 3rd trimester or postnatal)
1.2 Physical Examination
General: asymmetric hip abduction = suggestive unilateral dislocation. Hip abduction of 75 degrees should be possible in most newborns
Galeazzi sign: supine, hips + knees flexed to 90°, compare knee heights → one femur appears shorter → posterior dislocation. Bilateral DDH → Galeazzi NEGATIVE + symmetric abduction (อย่าพลาด!)

rows: Palpable clunk as femoral head reduces into acetabulum (abduct + lift)
Dislocate a LOCATED but unstable hip
Palpable clunk/posterior movement (adduct + posteriorly directed pressure)
IT band snapping over greater trochanter

blocks.text:
⚠️ Ortolani/Barlow ใช้ไม่ได้หลัง 3 เดือน
With progressive soft tissue contractures and loss of ligamentous laxity, both tests become unreliable after ~3 months of age. ดังนั้น ตรวจตั้งแต่แรกคลอด. หลัง 3 เดือน: limitation of hip abduction เป็น most reliable finding.
1.3 Imaging
Ultrasound: method of choice ก่อน 4–6 เดือน (femoral head = all cartilage; static anatomy + dynamic stability)
Ultrasound ก่อน 6 สัปดาห์: ดี สำหรับ confirm equivocal exam หรือ known dislocation monitoring; แต่ often reveals physiologic immaturity → overtreatment
AP radiograph pelvis: gold standard หลัง 6 เดือน (secondary ossification center ปรากฏ); ยาก interpret ก่อน 4–5 เดือน
1.4 AAOS Screening Guidelines (2014/2015)
Universal US screening: moderate evidence → do NOT perform routine universal US in all newborns
Risk factors (breech, family history, clinical instability): moderate evidence → imaging study before 6 months
Cochrane review (Shorter 2013): neither universal nor targeted US screening proven to improve clinical outcomes (late DDH rate, surgery)
USPSTF: 'insufficient evidence' for any routine screening including PE — controversial; current practice = universal PE screening + selective imaging
1.5 Treatment
Age 0–6 months, reducible hip: Pavlik harness — dynamic orthosis, allows active hip motion → deepens + stabilizes acetabulum. Apply ASAP after diagnosis
Double diapering: NOT accepted treatment for DDH

rows: Abandon harness if not reduced in 4 weeks

blocks.text: Persistently irreducible หรือ late presentation (>6 months): closed or open reduction under GA + spica casting
2. Foot Deformities
2.1 Classification
Toes pointing UP: calcaneovalgus, congenital vertical talus (CVT)
Toes pointing IN (medial deviation): metatarsus adductus, clubfoot (talipes equinovarus)
Extra/fused toes: polydactyly, syndactyly
2.2 Calcaneovalgus
Postural deformity: dorsum of foot can be directly opposed to anterior leg; plantar flexion limited
Incidence: 0.4–1 per 1000; more common in girls + breech deliveries
Association with DDH → ตรวจ hip ทุกราย!
Treatment: gentle parental stretching → spontaneous resolution 3–6 months; severe (cannot plantar flex past neutral) → serial casting
2.3 Congenital Vertical Talus (CVT)
Rarer; frequently associated with neuromuscular conditions + syndromes (arthrogryposis, spina bifida)
Pathology: hindfoot fixed in equinus + dorsal dislocation of midfoot at talonavicular joint → 'rocker-bottom' foot appearance
Treatment: serial casting to stretch dorsal soft tissues + reduce midfoot → limited surgical release if needed + Achilles tenotomy
Most require surgery 6–12 months; best outcomes if surgery < 2 years
2.4 Metatarsus Adductus
Incidence: 1 in 100 births; thought to result from intrauterine crowding
Features: concave medial border, curved lateral border, 'bean-shaped' sole, higher arch, neutral heel
Classification: passively correctable → observe, spontaneous improvement; non-passively correctable → serial casting by 6–9 months
Surgery: only children >3 years, rigid deformity, failed casting
2.5 Clubfoot (Talipes Equinovarus)
Definition: hindfoot equinus + heel varus + forefoot adduction and supination
Incidence: 1–2 per 1000 live births; risk factor: early amniocentesis (11–13 wks gestation) → ↓ fetal movement during critical foot development
Idiopathic clubfoot: deep single medial skin crease + curved lateral border + high arch + rigid varus/equinus heel + 'down and in' position
Spectrum: mild postural (passively correctable, no calf atrophy) → classic idiopathic → arthrogrypotic/neuromuscular (severe rigidity)

Ponseti Method — Gold Standard for Clubfoot
ทุก clubfoot ต้องส่ง pediatric orthopedist. Ponseti method: (1) Serial manipulations + long-leg casts q5–7 days, avg 4–5 casts in idiopathic; (2) Percutaneous Achilles tenotomy (most patients) + 3 more weeks cast; (3) Foot abduction orthosis full-time 3 months then part-time while sleeping until ~age 4 years. เริ่มภายในสัปดาห์แรก แต่ successful treatment ถึง 1 ปีได้. Recurrence rate 16–37% — ต้องติดตาม.
3. Torticollis
3.1 Congenital Muscular Torticollis (CMT)
Incidence: 0.3–2.0% of live births; usually discovered 6–8 weeks after birth
Presentation: 'cock robin' appearance — head tilted TOWARD + chin rotated AWAY from affected SCM
20–30% have palpable pseudotumor in middle-inferior SCM → spontaneously regresses → fibrous band. >50% have facial asymmetry or plagiocephaly
Left = right SCM affected equally. Cause: ischemia within SCM → fibrosis; intrauterine crowding → association with DDH + metatarsus adductus
3 Types: (1) Pseudotumor CMT; (2) Muscular torticollis (SCM fibrosis, no mass); (3) Postural torticollis (no contracture/fibrosis — NO surgery needed)
3.2 Treatment of CMT
Manual stretching program: rotate chin toward affected SCM + tilt head away; 10 reps × 10-count hold, ≥10×/day. Excellent results if started < 1 year
If no improvement in 3–4 wks → add physical therapist 2–3×/week; configure crib/toys to encourage rotation toward affected side
Surgery: NOT before 1 year + minimum 6 months PT failure. Cheng JBJS 2001 (n=821): only 8% with pseudotumor + 3% without required surgery.
Surgery: bipolar release/lengthening of SCM. Risk factors for surgery: late presentation, pseudotumor, rotation deficit >15°
Timing of surgery: significant plagiocephaly → before 2 years; mild asymmetry → good results up to 6 years
3.3 Other Causes of Torticollis
Klippel-Feil Syndrome (KFS): congenital osseous fusion cervical spine; triad: low posterior hairline + short webbed neck + limited ROM; genes: MEOX1 (AR KFS2), GDF3 (AD KFS3), MYO18B (AR KFS4)
Associations: congenital heart disease 14–29%, renal anomalies 25–35%, scoliosis 60%, audiologic anomalies 80%, deafness 15–35%, synkinesis 15–20%
Management: periodic observation ± activity modification; surgery if progressive deformity
Sandifer syndrome: GER-related torticollis — intermittent, changes direction, no SCM tightness, normal radiographs
Hemiatlas: failure of formation of C1 portion → open-mouth odontoid view radiograph diagnoses it
Others: CNS tumors, syringomyelia → if radiographs normal → thorough neuro exam + neurology referral
4. Torsional and Angular Deformities of the Lower Extremities
4.1 Genu Varum — Physiologic vs Pathologic
Genu varum + internal tibial torsion = nearly universal in neonates; both should spontaneously resolve 2–3 years of age
Salenius & Vankka JBJS 1975: neonates mean +15° varus; maximal varus at 6 months → neutral at 18 months → max valgus 12° at 3–4 years → normal adult valgus at 7 years
Genu varum physiologic up to age 2 years; even >30° angulation can correct spontaneously. Treatment with orthosis NOT recommended
Red flags requiring X-ray: asymmetric deformities, short stature, varus persistent >2 years, progressive deformity, rhizomelic shortening (achondroplasia)
DDx genu varum: focal fibrocartilage dysplasia, skeletal dysplasias, OI, metabolic bone disease (vit D-resistant rickets), Blount disease (tibia vara) — bilateral 80%, cannot diagnose before 2 years
4.2 Tibial Bowing — 3 Types

rows: Calcaneovalgus foot; limb length discrepancy avg 4.1 cm at maturity
Resolves by 2 years; serial follow-up for epiphysiodesis timing
Congenital pseudarthrosis of tibia; NF1 in 40–80%
Clamshell AFO until skeletal maturity; surgery if fracture
Fibular hemimelia (absent fibula, absent lateral rays, knee/hip anomalies)

blocks.text:
Anterolateral Bowing → Think NF1
Anterolateral tibial bowing → associated with congenital pseudarthrosis of tibia + NF1 in 40–80% of cases. Incidence 1:140,000. ตรวจ cutaneous signs of NF1 (café-au-lait spots). Early referral: pediatric orthopedics + genetics. Natural history: fracture + nonunion → repeated surgery. Syme amputation sometimes has better psych + functional outcomes than multiple failed repairs.
4.3 Congenital Knee Dislocation
Rare — incidence 0.017 per 1000; quadriceps contracture likely
Associations: clubfoot, arthrogryposis, myelodysplasia, Larsen syndrome; ipsilateral hip dysplasia 70–100%
Knee hyperextended so severely foot may reach child's face. Treatment: manipulation + serial casting promptly after X-ray diagnosis; surgery if unresponsive, best at ~6 months
5. Congenital Vertebral Malformations
5.1 Overview
Incidence: 0.5–1 per 1000 live births
Syndromes: Klippel-Feil, Goldenhar, VATER/VACTERL sequence, spondylocostal dysostosis
Intrauterine exposures: maternal hyperglycemia, CO, antiepileptic drugs. Many remain asymptomatic throughout life
5.2 Classification: Failure of Formation vs Segmentation
Failure of formation: asymmetric vertebral body development → hemivertebra (wedge or complete)
Fully segmented hemivertebra (open disk spaces both sides) = greatest propensity for deformity
Unsegmented hemivertebra (fused to adjacent vertebrae) = less growth potential, less deformity
Failure of segmentation: bony bars between adjacent vertebrae; bilateral bars = 'block vertebrae' = minimal deformity potential
5.3 Risk of Progression (McMaster & Ohtsuka JBJS 1982)
Risk in order of severity: unilateral bar + contralateral hemivertebra > unilateral bar > hemivertebra > wedge vertebra > block vertebra
Location: lumbosacral + cervicothoracic junctions → more clinically apparent deformities
5.4 Associated Anomaly Screening
Renal anomalies: 37% of children with congenital spinal anomalies → often clinically silent → routine renal US in ALL
Cardiac anomalies: ~15% — usually evident on PE; routine echo only if clinical findings suggestive
Occult intraspinal anomalies: up to 30% (Chiari, syringomyelia, tethered cord, diastematomyelia) → signs: skin dimpling, hairy patches, tags, foot deformities, asymmetric reflexes
Sprengel deformity: 50% of congenital cervical spine anomalies
Imaging: whole spine plain films first; MRI ถ้า going to surgery or neuro signs; US ตรวจ tethered cord ใน newborn; CT ไม่ routine (radiation)
6. Obstetric Trauma
6.1 Birth Fractures
Clavicle fracture: most common birth fracture; 2–3 per 1000 births. Risk factors: large baby, limited obstetrician experience, midforceps delivery
May be asymptomatic; symptomatic → pseudoparalysis of upper extremity. DDx: obstetric BPP, hematogenous osteomyelitis + septic arthritis
Diagnosis: X-ray; US useful for proximal humerus (entirely cartilaginous at birth)
Treatment: asymptomatic → observe; symptomatic → 7–10 days immobilization soft dressing
Femoral fracture: 0.13 per 1000 births; risk: twins, breech, prematurity, osteoporosis → treat with soft Pavlik harness; excellent outcomes, no deformity
6.2 Osteogenesis Imperfecta (OI) — Birth Fractures
Multiple long-bone + rib fractures at birth → think OI; prenatal diagnosis: US as early as 13–14 weeks

Sillence classification — perinatal types: Type II: generally lethal in neonatal period; Type III: survives to adulthood, considerable short stature + fracture morbidity; consider bisphosphonate treatment if multiple fractures + expected survival
Type III: respiratory failure = most common cause of death (rib fractures). Genetic consultation critical
Bruck syndrome: OI findings + joint contractures → FKBP10 mutations
6.3 Brachial Plexus Palsy (Obstetric)
Mechanism: lateral bending neck + shoulder depression → stretching/avulsion brachial plexus. Incidence 1–4 per 1000 live births
Risk factors: maternal diabetes, large birthweight, prolonged labor, forceps delivery, shoulder dystocia during vertex delivery; rarely cesarean

rows: Lost external rotation + abduction shoulder; hand preserved
Flaccid paralysis entire arm incl. hand
Hand weakness; may be recovered global injury

blocks.text:
Biceps Recovery at 3 Months = Critical Prognostic Landmark
Return of biceps function by 3 months = most important prognostic indicator. When biceps + shoulder abduction + wrist/finger extension return → 95% chance of normal function. ถ้า biceps recovery delayed >3 months → rare for complete recovery. Horner syndrome (ptosis, miosis, anhidrosis) = poor prognosis. Surgical exploration: total plexus + Horner + no biceps at 3 months; OR Erb + no biceps at 3–6 months. Surgery 3–6 months of age; exploration after 18 months of little benefit.
Treatment phase 1: PT/OT to avoid contractures during recovery observation. Only 1 in 10 require surgery
Primitive reflexes help assess: hand grasp (normal all neonates → fades 2–4 months); Moro (fades 3 months); ATNR/fencing (fades 4 months)
7. Neonatal Osteomyelitis and Septic Arthritis
7.1 Epidemiology
Septic arthritis incidence: 0.12 per 1000 live births; 0.67 per 1000 NICU admissions; mortality 7.3%
Most common joints: hip, knee, shoulder
Why neonates are vulnerable: immature immune response → susceptibility to organisms not ordinarily virulent + delays in expressing classic findings
7.2 Two Clinical Subgroups
Premature neonates requiring prolonged hospitalization: multiple peripheral/central catheters → organisms: S. aureus or gram-negative; multifocal in up to 40%; systemically ill

Healthy out-of-hospital newborn: 2–4 weeks after delivery → swelling + pseudoparalysis (no active limb movement) + tenderness; infant feeds well, NOT systemically ill; organisms: S. aureus + group B Streptococcus
7.3 Diagnosis
Neonates frequently do NOT demonstrate fever, leukocytosis, or elevated ESR due to immature immune response
CRP: reliable indicator — negative predictive value 95%; Laborada Am J Perinatol 2003
Blood culture: positive only 50%; synovial aspirate identifies organism only 30%

Imaging: plain X-ray first (bone changes absent ≤1 week); ultrasound: first-line — detects joint effusions, subperiosteal/soft tissue fluid, guides aspiration; MRI: highly sensitive, required if still suspected + negative US/X-ray (requires sedation)
7.4 Pathophysiology — Why Hip/Shoulder Are High Risk

Neonates: before secondary ossification center, epiphysis receives blood from metaphyseal vessels that cross the growth plate. Metaphyseal osteomyelitis → traverse growth plate → epiphyseal abscess → rupture into joint
Septic arthritis = sequela of adjacent osteomyelitis in up to 76% of neonates. Hip + shoulder: intra-articular metaphysis → direct decompression route into joint
Consequences: destruction of growth plate + articular cartilage → growth disturbances + precocious arthritis
7.5 Treatment
Aspiration: confirm diagnosis + culture → guide antibiotics
Osteomyelitis without abscess: antibiotics alone
Septic arthritis + subperiosteal/bone abscess: ALWAYS drain surgically in OR

⚠️ Unrecognized Hip Sepsis = Femoral Head Destruction
Delayed diagnosis ของ neonatal hip septic arthritis → complete dissolution of femoral head. ดังนั้น pseudoparalysis ของ extremity ใน neonate = ต้อง rule out septic arthritis ก่อนเสมอ. ส่ง US hip ทันที; ถ้า effusion → aspirate; ถ้า pus → drain surgically. อย่ารอ culture กลับ.
8. NICU Practical — เจอ newborn orthopedic problem ทำอะไร?
DDH exam ทุกราย: Ortolani + Barlow + Galeazzi; ถ้า positive clunk → เตรียม referral orthopedics; ตรวจ hip abduction (normal ≥75°). Click ≠ clunk — high-pitched click = IT band, benign
Risk factors DDH: breech + family history → US ที่ 4–6 สัปดาห์ แม้ exam negative
Clubfoot: ส่ง pediatric orthopedics เพื่อ Ponseti method ก่อน discharge หรือ ภายในสัปดาห์แรก. อย่าบอกพ่อแม่ว่า double-diaper จะช่วย
CMT: ตรวจ SCM ทุก newborn — pseudotumor? cock-robin posture? สอน stretching parents ทันที; ถ้าไม่ดีขึ้น 3–4 wks → PT referral

Pseudoparalysis ของ upper extremity: (1) ตรวจ clavicle (fracture + deformity + pain on palpation); (2) check Moro + ATNR reflex ทั้ง 2 ข้าง (3) ถ้า brachial plexus palsy suspected → track biceps recovery monthly; ถ้าไม่ recovery ที่ 3 เดือน → referral pediatric neurosurgery/orthopedics
Multiple rib + long bone fractures: think OI → genetics consult; prenatal US มักวินิจฉัยได้แล้ว
Pseudoparalysis ของ limb ใน 2–4 wks: rule out septic arthritis — ส่ง US ทันที; CRP NPV 95%; normal CRP ช่วย rule out แต่ positive = ต้อง pursue
Torticollis ที่ไม่ดีขึ้นด้วย stretching: X-ray cervical spine (AP + lateral + open-mouth odontoid); นึกถึง Klippel-Feil, hemiatlas, CNS tumor
9. 5 Bedside Pearls — สำหรับพรุ่งนี้

PEARL 1 — Bilateral DDH = Galeazzi Negative
กับดัก DDH: bilateral hip dislocation → Galeazzi sign NEGATIVE + symmetric hip abduction. ดังนั้น Galeazzi negative ≠ rule out DDH ถ้า bilateral. ต้องพึ่ง Ortolani + Barlow ทั้ง 2 ข้าง ทุกราย. และ click ≠ clunk — IT band clicks ไม่มีนัยสำคัญทางคลินิก.

PEARL 2 — Calcaneovalgus ≠ CVT: Hindfoot Is The Key
ทั้งสองเป็น 'dorsiflexed foot' แต่ต่างกันมาก: Calcaneovalgus: hindfoot FREE (not in equinus) + passively correctable + benign. CVT: hindfoot FIXED in equinus + rocker-bottom sole + associated with NMD/syndromes + requires serial casting + surgery. ตรวจ: ลองก้ม plantar flex hindfoot — ถ้า fixed = CVT.

PEARL 3 — Biceps at 3 Months = Surgery Decision Point for BPP
Obstetric brachial plexus palsy: ตรวจ biceps function ทุกเดือน จนถึง 3 เดือน. No biceps recovery at 3 months = indication ให้ทำ surgical exploration (+ Horner syndrome, + total plexus). Surgery window: 3–6 months; exploration after 18 months = little benefit. Waters JBJS 1999: microsurgical repair >> natural history ใน cases ที่ไม่มี biceps recovery.

PEARL 4 — Anterolateral Tibial Bowing → NF1 Until Proven Otherwise
Anterolateral bowing ≠ benign like posteromedial. Anterolateral = associated with congenital pseudarthrosis of tibia + NF1 in 40–80%. ตรวจ café-au-lait spots ทันที; refer orthopedics + genetics เร็ว. Natural history: fracture → nonunion → repeated surgery → may end in Syme amputation (which can give better functional outcome than multiple failed procedures).

PEARL 5 — Neonatal Pseudoparalysis = Septic Arthritis Until Proven Otherwise
Newborn 2–4 wks อายุ มี pseudoparalysis (ไม่ขยับแขนหรือขา) + swelling/tenderness แต่ ไม่มีไข้, WBC ปกติ, ESR ปกติ ≠ rule out septic arthritis ใน neonates (immature immune response). ส่ง CRP + US ทันที; CRP NPV 95% = ถ้า negative ช่วย rule out. ถ้า US effusion → aspirate + culture + drain surgically ถ้า pus.
10. Evidence Update — Landmark Studies

Salenius P, Vankka E. JBJS Am 1975;57:259: Tibiofemoral angle development in 979 children — newborns mean +15° varus, resolves to neutral at 18 months, max valgus 12° at 3–4 years, adult alignment by 7 years. Defines physiologic genu varum.

Shorter D, Hong T, Osborn DA. Cochrane Review. Evid Based Child Health 2013;8:11: Screening programs for DDH in newborns — neither universal nor targeted US screening demonstrated to improve clinical outcomes including late DDH or surgery rates.

Mulpuri K et al. (AAOS). JBJS Am 2015;97:1717: AAOS evidence-based guideline on detection and nonoperative management of pediatric DDH up to 6 months — 9 specific recommendations including selective US screening for risk factors and Pavlik harness as first-line treatment.

Ponseti IV. JBJS Am 1992;74:448: Ponseti method for congenital clubfoot — serial manipulation and casting with excellent mid- to long-term results and decreased stiffness vs surgery. Foundation of modern clubfoot management.

Faulks S, Richards BS. Clin Orthop Relat Res 2009;467:1278: Clubfoot treatment: Ponseti and French functional methods are equally effective — both nonoperative methods have equivalent outcomes; Ponseti preferred for lower resource requirements.

Cheng JC et al. JBJS Am 2001;83:679: Prospective study of 821 children with CMT — manual stretching resulted in only 8% (pseudotumor) and 3% (no pseudotumor) requiring surgery. Risk factors for surgery: late presentation, pseudotumor, >15° rotation deficit.

McMaster MJ, Ohtsuka K. JBJS Am 1982;64:1128: Natural history of congenital scoliosis in 251 patients — classification of defect type and risk of progression; unilateral bar + contralateral hemivertebra = highest risk.

Waters PM. JBJS Am 1999;81:649: Comparison of natural history vs microsurgical repair vs operative reconstruction in brachial plexus birth palsy — microsurgical repair superior to natural history in cases without biceps recovery; surgical window 3–6 months.

Michelow BJ et al. Plast Reconstr Surg 1994;93:675: Natural history of obstetric BPP — return of biceps by 3 months + shoulder abduction + wrist/finger extension = 95% chance of normal function. Late biceps recovery → rarely complete.
Laborada G et al. Am J Perinatol 2003;20:491: CRP in first 24 hours of neonatal sepsis — negative predictive value 95% for AHO/septic arthritis; CRP more reliable than WBC or ESR in neonates.

Rubin LG et al. J Pediatr 2020;227:157 (3 NICUs): Frequency of multifocal disease and hip pyogenic arthritis in neonates with osteoarticular infection — up to 40% multifocal in NICU premature neonates; S. aureus most common.

Herring JA et al. JBJS Am 1986;68:573: Syme amputation for congenital pseudarthrosis of tibia — children with Syme amputation had better psychological and orthopedic functioning than those undergoing multiple corrective procedures.

van Wijk RM et al. BMJ 2014;348:g2741: Helmet therapy in infants with positional skull deformation — RCT: helmet therapy not more effective than natural course for plagiocephaly; possibly detrimental in some. Controversies remain.

Nadas S et al. Skeletal Radiol 1993;22:195: Predisposing factors in obstetrical fractures — long-bone fractures associated with cesarean delivery, breech delivery with assistance, and low birth weight.
11. Self-Check Questions พร้อมเฉลย
Q: DDH exam: อธิบาย Ortolani test ขั้นตอน และอธิบาย Barlow test — ทำไมต้องทั้ง 2 test?

ตอบ: Ortolani: supine, flex hip + knee 90°, fingers on greater trochanter + thumb medial thigh; abduct hip + lift gently → positive = palpable clunk (dislocated hip reduces). Barlow: same setup; adduct hip + gentle posterior pressure on knee → positive = clunk/posterior movement (located but unstable hip dislocates). ต้องทั้ง 2 test: Ortolani จับ dislocated hip แต่ Barlow จับ unstable hip ที่ยังไม่ dislocate — คนละ pathology ในหนึ่ง spectrum. High-pitched click = IT band, benign.
Q: ทารกหญิง term breech presentation ตรวจ Ortolani/Barlow negative — ต้องทำ imaging ไหม?

ตอบ: ใช่ — breech presentation + female sex = risk factors สำหรับ DDH. AAOS 2014: moderate evidence supports imaging study before 6 months ใน infants with risk factors (breech, family history, clinical instability) แม้ exam stable. Recommended: ultrasound at 4–6 weeks of age (ก่อน 4 สัปดาห์อาจ overdiagnose physiologic immaturity). Universal US screening ไม่แนะนำ (Cochrane 2013).
Q: Newborn มี foot ที่ dorsum แตะ tibia ได้, plantar flexion จำกัด — diagnosis คืออะไร? treatment?

ตอบ: Diagnosis: calcaneovalgus — postural deformity, dorsum of foot directly opposes anterior leg, plantar flexion limited. ตรวจ hip ด้วย (associated with DDH). Treatment: mild → gentle parental stretching; spontaneous resolution 3–6 months. Severe (cannot plantar flex past neutral) → serial casting. ต้องแยกจาก CVT: CVT มี hindfoot fixed in equinus + rocker-bottom sole — ต้องการ serial casting + surgery 6–12 months.
Q: Ponseti method for clubfoot: ขั้นตอน, Achilles tenotomy indication, และ bracing timeline?

ตอบ: Ponseti method: (1) Serial manipulation + long-leg casts q5–7 days, avg 4–5 casts for idiopathic clubfoot; (2) percutaneous Achilles tenotomy in most patients + 3 more weeks cast; (3) foot abduction orthosis full-time 3 months then part-time sleeping until ~age 4 years. เริ่มเร็วที่สุด แต่ effective ถึง 1 ปี. Recurrence 16–37% → further casting; 8–16% require hindfoot surgical release. Ponseti = French method = equally effective (Faulks 2009).
Q: CMT: อธิบาย 3 subtypes และ risk factors สำหรับ surgery?

ตอบ: 3 subtypes: (1) Pseudotumor CMT: palpable SCM mass, spontaneously regresses; (2) Muscular torticollis: SCM fibrosis without mass; (3) Postural torticollis: no contracture/fibrosis — NO surgery ever needed. Risk factors for surgery: late initial presentation, presence of pseudotumor, rotation deficit >15°. Surgery: NOT before 1 year + ≥6 months PT failure. Cheng 2001: 8% with pseudotumor, 3% without required surgery after structured stretching.
Q: Klippel-Feil syndrome: triad, genetic subtypes, associated anomalies ที่ต้อง screen?

ตอบ: Triad: low posterior hairline + short webbed neck + limited cervical ROM. Genetic: MEOX1 (AR KFS2), GDF3 (AD KFS3), MYO18B (AR KFS4); most cases no genetic etiology. Associations: cardiac 14–29%, renal 25–35%, scoliosis 60%, audiologic 80%, deafness 15–35%, synkinesis 15–20%. Recognition of KFS = must screen for these. ส่ง echo + renal US + hearing screen + ophthalmology. Natural history usually favorable; severe cases → spondylosis, progressive torticollis, neurologic compromise.
Q: Congenital vertebral malformation: renal screening ทำไมจึงสำคัญ? cardiac ต้อง screen อย่างไร?

ตอบ: Renal anomalies: 37% of children with congenital spinal anomalies — often clinically silent → routine renal US in ALL. Cardiac anomalies: ~15% — usually evident on PE → routine echo NOT recommended unless clinical findings suggestive. Occult intraspinal anomalies: 30% (tethered cord, syringomyelia, Chiari) → ตรวจ cutaneous signs (dimples, hairy patches, skin tags) + foot deformities + asymmetric reflexes → US spine ใน newborn ช่วย detect tethered cord.
Q: Neonatal brachial plexus palsy: Erb vs Klumpke, และเมื่อไหร่ส่ง surgery?

ตอบ: Erb palsy (C5–C6): lost external rotation + abduction shoulder, hand PRESERVED. Global plexus (C5–T1): flaccid paralysis entire arm including hand. Klumpke (C8–T1): hand weakness only; often recovered global. Horner syndrome (ptosis/miosis/anhidrosis) = poor prognosis. Surgery indication: total plexus + Horner + no biceps at 3 months; OR Erb (C5–C6) + no biceps at 3–6 months. Surgery: 3–6 months of age. Waters 1999: microsurgical repair > natural history. Surgical exploration after 18 months = little benefit.
Q: Neonatal septic arthritis: ทำไม fever และ leukocytosis ไม่น่าเชื่อถือ? diagnostic approach?

ตอบ: Neonates have immature immune response → frequently no fever, no leukocytosis, no elevated ESR. CRP = most reliable: negative predictive value 95% (Laborada 2003). Blood culture positive only 50%; synovial aspirate identifies organism only 30%. Approach: CRP + US hip/knee/shoulder; effusion detected → aspirate; pus → drain surgically in OR. Osteomyelitis without abscess → antibiotics alone. Key: septic arthritis = sequela of adjacent osteomyelitis in up to 76% of neonates — metaphyseal vessels cross growth plate.
Q: Anterolateral tibial bowing ต่างจาก posteromedial อย่างไร? ทั้งคู่มี natural history แบบไหน?

ตอบ: Posteromedial: associated with calcaneovalgus foot; resolves by 2 years; shortening persists (avg 4.1 cm leg length discrepancy at maturity) → follow-up for epiphysiodesis timing; no NF1 association. Anterolateral: NF1 in 40–80%; associated with congenital pseudarthrosis of tibia; natural history = fracture + nonunion → AFO until skeletal maturity; multiple surgical attempts commonly fail; Syme amputation sometimes best outcome (Herring 1986). ส่ง pediatric orthopedics + genetics ทันที.
Day 90 Preview

Avery Chapter 90 — Skeletal Dysplasias and Heritable Connective Tissue Disorders
พรุ่งนี้ต่อจาก orthopedic conditions สู่ Skeletal Dysplasias — กลุ่มโรคที่เกิดจาก defects ใน bone/cartilage formation: achondroplasia (FGFR3), osteogenesis imperfecta (COL1A1/2), thanatophoric dysplasia, Marfan syndrome. สำคัญ: achondroplasia = most common non-lethal skeletal dysplasia; วันนี้เราเรียน OI เพียงผิวเผิน — พรุ่งนี้จะลงรายละเอียดด้าน genetics, prognosis, bisphosphonates, foramen magnum compression ใน achondroplasia.
